content.text: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is the first training stage after pre-training that teaches a language model to follow instructions and engage in dialogue. A pre-trained base model has learned to predict the next token across a vast corpus, but it has no notion of being helpful, following instructions, or maintaining a conversational format. SFT bridges this gap by training on a curated dataset of
, showing the model the desired input–output behaviour directly.
In the canonical RLHF pipeline, SFT is stage 1 of 3:
— train on demonstration data to get an instruction-following model (SFT model).
— train a reward model on human preference comparisons between SFT outputs.
— fine-tune the SFT model using the RM signal via PPO or DPO.
The LIMA paper demonstrated that as few as 1 000 high-quality demonstrations can yield strong instruction-following, a finding known as the
: the model already contains most of the required knowledge; SFT teaches the style and format of communication.

attrs.alt: SFT pipeline: raw dataset → tokenise+template → SFT training → evaluation → save/deploy, with chat template masking detail and tooling comparison

content.text: Models do not receive raw text; they receive structured sequences produced by a
. A chat template converts a list of messages (system, user, assistant turns) into a single string with special tokens that delimit role boundaries. Llama-3 uses:

<|begin_of_text|><|start_header_id|>system<|end_header_id|>

You are a helpful assistant.<|eot_id|><|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>

What is gradient descent?<|eot_id|><|start_header_id|>assistant<|end_header_id|>

Gradient descent is an optimisation algorithm...<|eot_id|>
to handle this automatically and consistently across models.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct")

messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
    {"role": "user",   "content": "What is gradient descent?"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "Gradient descent is an optimisation algorithm..."},
]

# add_generation_prompt=False because we include the full assistant turn
formatted = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
    messages,
    tokenize=False,
    add_generation_prompt=False,
)
print(formatted)
only compute loss on the assistant's tokens
. Computing loss on system or user tokens would train the model to predict prompts it will never have to generate, which wastes capacity and can confuse the model. The standard approach is to set
for all prompt tokens; PyTorch's cross-entropy loss ignores

def tokenize_with_masking(example, tokenizer, max_length=2048):
    """Mask all prompt tokens so loss is computed only on the response."""
    prompt = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
        example["messages"][:-1],          # everything except final assistant turn
        tokenize=False,
        add_generation_prompt=True,         # append the opening assistant header
    )
    full = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
        example["messages"],
        tokenize=False,
        add_generation_prompt=False,
    )

    prompt_ids = tokenizer(prompt, add_special_tokens=False)["input_ids"]
    full_ids   = tokenizer(full,   add_special_tokens=False)["input_ids"]

    # Pad/truncate to max_length
    input_ids = full_ids[:max_length]
    labels    = [-100] * len(prompt_ids) + full_ids[len(prompt_ids):max_length]

    return {"input_ids": input_ids, "labels": labels,
            "attention_mask": [1] * len(input_ids)}
as its default. Tokens labelled -100 contribute zero gradient, effectively removing them from the optimisation objective without any special-casing in the training loop.
with automatic chat-template application, loss masking, sequence packing, and LoRA integration. It is the recommended starting point for most SFT jobs.

from datasets import load_dataset
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, TrainingArguments
from trl import SFTTrainer, SFTConfig

model_id = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B"
model     = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_id, torch_dtype="auto", device_map="auto")
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16,
    lora_alpha=32,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj", "k_proj", "o_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none",
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)

dataset = load_dataset("HuggingFaceH4/ultrachat_200k", split="train_sft")

training_args = SFTConfig(
    output_dir="./sft-llama3-8b",
    num_train_epochs=2,
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=4,       # effective batch = 16
    learning_rate=2e-4,
    lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
    warmup_ratio=0.05,
    bf16=True,
    logging_steps=10,
    save_strategy="epoch",
    evaluation_strategy="epoch",
    max_seq_length=2048,
    packing=True,                        # pack short sequences into one 2k context
    dataset_text_field="messages",
    report_to="wandb",
)

trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=dataset,
)
trainer.train()
Truncate sequences; longer = more GPU memory
Concatenate short examples to fill context window, improving GPU utilisation
Simulate larger batch without extra memory
Fraction of steps to linearly ramp LR from 0
Lower for full fine-tune vs LoRA
Higher r → more capacity, more memory
Monitoring an SFT run requires watching both optimisation signals and generalisation proxies.
Tracks train loss, slight gap is normal
Eval loss rises while train loss falls → overfit
Sustained norm > 10 suggests LR too high
< 50 % means data loading is the bottleneck
Drops abruptly → GPU thermal throttle or OOM swap
to capture per-layer gradient norms, which helps diagnose which layers are learning vs. frozen.
Loss alone is an unreliable quality signal; strong SFT practitioners use a three-tier evaluation stack:
— eval loss on a held-out split. Fast, continuous, but measures distribution fit rather than task quality. Use for early stopping.
— accuracy on classification prompts, BLEU/ROUGE for summarisation, pass@k for code. Run at the end of each epoch with
— MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, or IFEval probe open-ended quality. LLM-as-judge (GPT-4 scoring) gives a scalable proxy for human evaluation.

# Run lm-evaluation-harness against your SFT checkpoint
lm_eval \
  --model hf \
  --model_args pretrained=./sft-llama3-8b \
  --tasks mmlu,hellaswag,arc_easy \
  --device cuda:0 \
  --batch_size 8 \
  --output_path ./eval_results.json
Flexible Python API; easy to extend; integrates DPO/PPO/ORPO in same library
Less configuration than YAML-based tools; no built-in multi-node
Opinionated defaults; flash-attention, DeepSpeed, FSDP built in; active community
Production fine-tuning across many model families
Web UI; supports 100+ models; LoRA/QLoRA/DoRA; one-command deploy
Heavier dependency footprint; CLI flags can be verbose
Custom CUDA kernels; 2–5× faster than TRL on a single GPU; 60 % less VRAM
Limited multi-GPU; model coverage lags newest releases
SFT datasets are typically small (1 k – 200 k examples), making overfitting a genuine risk after even 2–3 passes.
— sufficient when the dataset is large (> 100 k) or highly diverse. Model barely touches each example once; generalisation is often strongest.
— sweet spot for most production SFT jobs (10 k – 100 k examples). Monitor eval loss per epoch and stop early if it starts rising.
— only justified for very small, high-quality datasets (< 5 k examples) with explicit regularisation (weight decay 0.1, dropout 0.05).
Implement early stopping by tracking best eval loss and restoring the best checkpoint at the end:

from transformers import EarlyStoppingCallback

trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=SFTConfig(
        ...,
        evaluation_strategy="steps",
        eval_steps=200,
        save_strategy="steps",
        save_steps=200,
        load_best_model_at_end=True,
        metric_for_best_model="eval_loss",
        greater_is_better=False,
    ),
    callbacks=[EarlyStoppingCallback(early_stopping_patience=3)],
    train_dataset=train_ds,
    eval_dataset=eval_ds,
)
When your dataset contains many short examples (< 200 tokens), padding them individually to
wastes 80–90 % of each GPU computation on padding tokens.
concatenates examples with an EOS separator until the buffer is full, producing densely-filled context windows:

# With packing=True in SFTConfig, TRL handles this automatically.
# Internally it uses ConstantLengthDataset:
from trl import ConstantLengthDataset

packed_dataset = ConstantLengthDataset(
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    dataset=dataset,
    dataset_text_field="text",
    seq_length=2048,
    eos_token_id=tokenizer.eos_token_id,
    shuffle=True,
)
# The packed dataset yields batches with virtually zero padding waste.

Packing concatenates multiple examples into one sequence. Without special masking, the model's attention can span across example boundaries, letting later examples attend to earlier ones. TRL's packing implements cross-sample attention masking to prevent this, but not all implementations do — check the tool's documentation before using packing.
Overfit to repetitive training data or too many epochs
Reduce epochs, increase dataset diversity, add repetition penalty
Labels not masked; model is trained on inputs too
Verify mask: check that labels[prompt_tokens] are all -100
All tokens in an eval batch are masked (-100)
Filter out examples with no assistant tokens; check masking logic
System prompt missing from training template
Ensure every training example includes the system role in the message list
Sequence length × batch size too large
Halve batch size, add gradient checkpointing, switch to QLoRA
Prefer LoRA; lower LR; add replay data from the pre-training distribution
Saving and Deploying the SFT Model
After training completes, merge LoRA adapters into the base weights for single-model deployment, then push to the Hugging Face Hub:

# Load the trained PEFT model
from peft import AutoPeftModelForCausalLM
import torch

peft_model = AutoPeftModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "./sft-llama3-8b",
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="cpu",             # merge on CPU to avoid OOM
)

# Merge LoRA weights into the base model and drop the adapter structure
merged = peft_model.merge_and_unload()

# Save the merged model
merged.save_pretrained("./sft-llama3-8b-merged")
tokenizer.save_pretrained("./sft-llama3-8b-merged")

# Optional: push to Hub
merged.push_to_hub("your-org/sft-llama3-8b")
tokenizer.push_to_hub("your-org/sft-llama3-8b")
The merged model can then be quantised to INT4/INT8 with bitsandbytes or llama.cpp GGUF format for CPU inference, or served directly via vLLM, TGI, or Ollama.
SFT is the first post-pre-training stage: it uses demonstration data to teach instruction-following format and dialogue style.
Chat templates structure the token sequence; loss masking (labels = -100 for prompt tokens) ensures only the assistant response is trained.
TRL SFTTrainer with LoRA is the fastest path to a working fine-tune; Axolotl and LLaMA-Factory offer YAML-driven production workflows.
Sequence packing removes padding waste; early stopping prevents overfitting on small datasets; 2–3 epochs is typical.
Evaluate beyond loss: use lm-evaluation-harness for capability benchmarks and LLM-as-judge for instruction-following quality.
Merge LoRA adapters with merge_and_unload() before deployment to reduce serving overhead.
